title: Pumice gardener's scrub bar
subtitle: A heavy-duty cold-process bar with pumice, tea tree, and peppermint for working hands
excerpt: Pumice granules embedded in a coconut-oil-heavy cold-process base give this bar enough abrasive action to shift ingrained soil and plant sap from the hands. Tea tree and peppermint essential oils add a clean scent and known cleansing properties. Hard and long-lasting in use.
sourceNotes: SAP values: coconut oil 0.190, olive oil 0.134, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat applied. Pumice as soap exfoliant: Anne Bramson, Soap: Making It, Enjoying It (1972), pp. 67-70. Tea tree antimicrobial activity: Carson et al., Clinical Microbiology Reviews (2006). Peppermint EO safety at 1% in rinse-off products: IFRA standard (2019).

recipe.batchNotes: Scale by running revised oil weights through a lye calculator. Do not multiply the lye weight directly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cold-process soap requires 48 hours in the mould before unmoulding and 4 to 6 weeks to cure.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Cured bars keep two years in a cool dark place. Discard if the bar develops orange spots or a rancid smell. The pumice surface remains gritty throughout the bar's life.

glossaryTerms.definition: The reaction between fat and lye that produces soap. Each oil has a SAP value giving the grams of lye needed to fully saponify one gram of that oil.
The point at which the soap batter emulsifies to a custard-like consistency. Pumice and additives are stirred in at trace.

content.text: A bar designed for hands that spend time in the garden or workshop. The oil base uses a higher coconut oil percentage than most everyday bars, giving a firm, quick-lathering result with good soil-cutting power. Fine pumice granules stirred in at
add mechanical scrubbing without scratching. Tea tree and peppermint essential oils give the bar a fresh scent; both have well-documented cleansing properties in rinse-off applications.

items.prepNote: 50% of oil weight, for hardness and lather
5% of oil weight, for stable lather
fine grade; stirred in at trace
about 140 drops; added at light trace
about 60 drops; added at light trace

content.text: Work in a ventilated space. Wear safety goggles and chemical-resistant protective gloves. Weigh all ingredients and set out the pumice and essential oils before mixing the lye.

Add the sodium hydroxide to the cold distilled water in a steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never add water to lye. The solution heats to around 90 degrees C and releases fumes; do not lean over the pitcher. Cool to 38 to 42 degrees C.
Melt the coconut oil and combine with the olive and castor oils. Cool to 38 to 42 degrees C.
Blend to trace and add pumice

Pour the lye solution into the oils in a thin stream and stick-blend to light trace. Add the pumice powder and the essential oils and stir by hand until evenly distributed. Do not stick-blend after adding pumice; the granules can damage the blender seal. The mixture should be at a medium trace at this point.

Pour into the silicone mould. The pumice settles slightly; pour from a low height and tap the mould twice to even the distribution. Cover and wrap in a towel. Unmould after 48 hours. The high coconut oil percentage means this bar is firmer and easier to unmould than most. Cut into bars 2.5 cm thick and cure on a rack for 4 to 6 weeks.
continues during the cure; wear gloves when handling freshly cut bars.

Store on an open soap dish in a cool cupboard. The bar keeps two years. The pumice surface remains effective for the full life of the bar; no need to rotate or replace. Discard if the bar smells rancid or develops orange spots (DOS). The essential oils fade after the first year; the scrubbing action does not.
